title: Isaiah 39
subtitle: The Babylonian Envoys: The Bridge to Second Isaiah

heb.paragraph: "Hezekiah was pleased and showed them (herʾâ) all that was in his storehouses — the silver, the gold, the spices, the fine olive oil — his entire armoury and everything found among his treasures" (v.2). Merodach-Baladan of Babylon sends envoys with a gift, ostensibly for Hezekiah’s recovery. But Hezekiah’s response is catastrophic: he shows them EVERYTHING. Isaiah asks: "What did they see?" Hezekiah admits: "They saw everything; there is nothing among my treasures that I did not show them" (v.4). The king who trusted God against Assyria trusts Babylon with his security secrets. The fatal miscalculation that sets up the exile.

notes.note: "Merodach-Baladan sent letters and a gift." Diplomatic courtesy that conceals intelligence-gathering. Babylon is not yet the superpower; it is scouting.
"Hezekiah showed them everything." The verb is emphatic. The man who spread Sennacherib’s letter before God now spreads his treasures before Babylon. The contrast is devastating.
"There is nothing among my treasures that I did not show them." Full disclosure to the future enemy. The king who should have learned from ch.37 has already forgotten.

sections.header: Verses 5–8 — “Everything Will Be Carried Off to Babylon”

heb.paragraph: "Hear the word of the LORD: the time will surely come when everything in your palace will be carried off to Babylon (Bābel). Nothing will be left. And some of your descendants will be taken away, and they will become eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon" (vv.5–7). Isaiah’s verdict: everything you showed them, they will take. The treasure tour becomes the prophecy of exile. Hezekiah’s response is troubling: "‘The word of the LORD you have spoken is good,’ for he thought, ‘There will be peace and security in my lifetime’" (v.8). He accepts the verdict because it falls on his descendants, not on him. First Isaiah closes on this note of failure — setting up the need for the comfort of Second Isaiah (ch.40).

notes.note: "Everything will be carried off to Babylon. Some of your descendants will become eunuchs." The exile prophesied. Daniel and his friends (Dan 1) are the fulfilment: royal descendants serving in Babylon’s palace.

"The word of the LORD is good — there will be peace in MY lifetime." The most disappointing response in the Hezekiah narrative. The king who prayed magnificently in ch.37 now accepts exile because it won’t happen to him. Personal peace traded for generational disaster.

ppl.name: LORD
ppl.text: "The word of the LORD is good — there will be peace in MY lifetime…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVAt that time Marduk-Baladan son of Baladan king of Babylon sent Hezekiah letters and a gift, because he had heard of his illness and recovery.ESVAt that time Merodach-baladan the son of Baladan, king of Babylon, sent envoys with letters and a present to Hezekiah, for he heard that he had been sick and had recovered.NIV“The word of the Lord you have spoken is good,” Hezekiah replied. For he thought, “There will be peace and security in my lifetime.”ESVThen Hezekiah said to Isaiah, “The word of the LORD that you have spoken is good.” For he thought, “There will be peace and security in my days.”

rec.text: ""Merodach-Baladan sent letters and a gift." Diplomatic courtesy that conceals intelligence-gathering. Babylon is not yet the superpower; it is scouting."

rows.label: Carried Off to Babylon — 39:5–8
rows.text: "EVERYTHING carried off to Babylon." Hezekiah accepts it: "Peace in MY lifetime." FIRST ISAIAH CLOSES on failure.

hebtext.note: "Hezekiah was pleased and showed them (herʾâ) all that was in his storehouses — the silver, the gold, the spices, the fine olive oil — his entire armoury and everything found among his treasures" (v.2). Merodach-Baladan of Babylon sends envoys with a gift, ostensibly for Hezekiah’s recovery. But Hezekiah’s response is catastrophic: he shows them EVERYTHING. Isaiah asks: "What did they see?" Hezekiah admits: "They saw everything; there is nothing among my treasures that I did not show them" (v.4). The king who trusted God against Assyria trusts Babylon with his security secrets. The fatal miscalculation that sets up the exile.

"Hear the word of the LORD: the time will surely come when everything in your palace will be carried off to Babylon (Bābel). Nothing will be left. And some of your descendants will be taken away, and they will become eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon" (vv.5–7). Isaiah’s verdict: everything you showed them, they will take. The treasure tour becomes the prophecy of exile. Hezekiah’s response is troubling: "‘The word of the LORD you have spoken is good,’ for he thought, ‘There will be peace and security in my lifetime’" (v.8). He accepts the verdict because it falls on his descendants, not on him. First Isaiah closes on this note of failure — setting up the need for the comfort of Second Isaiah (ch.40).

themes.note: Prophecy peaks: the exile foretold. Daniel 1 fulfils this. First Isaiah closes on failure: the king who trusted God against Assyria trusts Babylon with everything. The bridge to "Comfort, comfort my people."
